claim: Assertions express extractable behavioral patterns as beliefs, not individual occurrences. Incidents that evidence the pattern belong in the evidence array, not as the claim itself.

evidence.detail: The user reframed the initial belief: state the behavior that can be extrapolated from an incident, so the recounted experience becomes evidence in an array rather than the claim itself. The claim captures the pattern; the evidence captures each incident. Multiple incidents strengthen the same belief rather than creating new ones.

claim: Compound assertions survive session boundaries - an implication created in March is still there in April, still linked to its deps, still flaggable if a dep changes. Without the DAG, every session re-reads raw data and reconstructs some fraction of the reasoning, losing compound insights that depend on having the right facts adjacent at the right time.

evidence.detail: Every session starts cold; the agent reads raw data and reconstructs reasoning from scratch. Compound beliefs survive sessions. Verified: beliefs created weeks earlier remained queryable and were composed into later compounds by subsequent sessions (e.g. a dependency fan-in detection).

claim: A centralized graph in a single file is preferred over per-entity belief files because cross-entity composition is the primary value. A primitive about one entity composes with a primitive about another to produce an implication - splitting by entity would fragment the graph. Subject-based queries provide per-entity views without separate files.

evidence.detail: Considered and rejected: per-entity belief files. The centralized graph was preserved because cross-entity composition is the primary value, subject-based queries already provide per-entity views, and the store and query tools assume a single file.

claim: Sources are not beliefs. A source document is raw material from which primitives are extracted. The graph stores structured beliefs; sources store evidence. The relationship is bidirectional - sources feed primitives upstream (a document becomes a primitive), and syntheses reference beliefs downstream (an analysis cites belief IDs). Both directions can be active on the same document.

evidence.detail: A source document is the raw material from which primitives are extracted; it is not itself a belief. Primitives carry source references and evidence arrays with source language. Source documents are archived as reference copies. No belief's claim field contains multi-paragraph prose.

claim: Multi-paragraph analyses should not be belief kinds. The schema is built around atomic, composable beliefs - the claim field is a one-liner. Stuffing prose into a belief inverts the relationship: the prose becomes the point and the claim becomes a title. Analyses live as source documents referencing belief IDs inline.

evidence.detail: The prose is the primary artifact, not a property of a one-line claim. Stuffing a multi-paragraph analysis into a belief inverts the relationship. All beliefs have atomic claims.

claim: Self-referential beliefs use the existing schema with subject type 'agent' - no special case needed. They capture agent operational patterns, failure modes, and collaboration dynamics using the same primitive/compound/implication structure as domain beliefs. Human-observed data (corrections, failure observations) produces the highest-quality self-referential primitives.

evidence.detail: The graph supports beliefs where the subject is the agent itself; these use the existing schema with no special case. Such beliefs include primitives (reasoning-error), compounds (error-pattern), and implications. The schema required zero modifications.

claim: A traversal-path belief presents a path through graph nodes rather than synthesizing deps into a single belief. The routing IS the argument - like a modular synthesizer patch. The conclusion is separable from the routing, enabling independent evaluation.

evidence.detail: A traversal-path belief differs from a compound in three ways: it presents a traversal where a compound synthesizes; its conclusion is separable from its routing, enabling independent evaluation; and its support is derived from downstream agreement rather than author assessment.

claim: Every primitive sourced from a document or email carries an evidence array with source details including dates and specific descriptions of what was observed. The claim is the agent's interpretation; the evidence carries the factual basis. The gap between claim and evidence is where misinterpretation lives and can be audited.

evidence.detail: Every primitive sourced from a document or message carries an evidence array with the source language. All primitives have evidence arrays. The claim is the agent's interpretation; the evidence carries the factual basis, and the gap between them is where misinterpretation lives and can be audited.

claim: Beliefs are created at extraction time - when the agent is actively reading a source or reasoning about entities - not backfilled retroactively. The exception is an explicit invocation to formalize prior reasoning from a conversation.

evidence.detail: Like provenance, beliefs are created when the agent is actively reading a source or reasoning about entities - not backfilled retroactively. Belief creation dates cluster around documented extraction sessions.

claim: Beliefs and contracts are two roles sharing one store. Ordinary beliefs become stale and get superseded. Contracts are behavioral invariants that get violated or upheld, not superseded. A belief says 'this is true'; a contract says 'this will remain true.'

evidence.detail: Beliefs versus contracts: ordinary beliefs carry statuses active/superseded/retracted; contracts are contract-grade implications that may additionally use retired. Both live in the same store.

claim: Field-level provenance (_provenance block on entities) maps source documents to the specific fields they substantiate. This makes the data model 100% auditable - if the agent tells you something, you can check the source. Errors become real-time hallucinations, not deep data mistakes.

evidence.detail: Field-level provenance blocks on entities map source paths to the field lists they substantiate. The pattern uses an underscore prefix so existing tooling ignores it. This makes the data model auditable: if the agent states something, the source can be checked.

claim: Provenance _source attribution must only be assigned at extraction time - when the agent is actively reading the source document and pulling data from it. Retroactive attribution ('this data probably came from that document') is unreliable and prohibited. Unsourced data gets 'manual' and must be re-extracted from a candidate source to be upgraded.

evidence.detail: Provenance attribution must only be assigned at extraction time, when the agent is actively reading the source. Retroactive attribution is unreliable and prohibited. This parallels the extraction-time belief-creation principle: claims about data sources must be made at the moment of reading, not after the fact.

claim: The extraction-time discipline applies across all data layers: provenance attribution happens when reading source documents (a125), assertions are created when reading sources or reasoning about entities (a119), and event log entries are written at mutation time (a128). Retroactive attribution is prohibited at every layer because post-hoc source assignment is inherently unreliable.

Contracts fill the abstraction gap between literal code and plain English for supervising agentic coding. The system had implicit contracts scattered across three representations: structs (shape without behavioral invariants), skills (workflow without guarantees to callers), and the agent-instruction file (policy without mechanical verification). Contracts make these guarantees explicit, machine-readable, and verifiable.

evidence.detail: Code and file trees are the wrong abstraction layer for a person supervising agentic coding: literal code is too low-level, plain English has no guarantees, contracts fill the gap. The system had implicit contracts scattered across structs (shape without behavioral invariants), skills (workflow without guarantees), and instruction files (policy without mechanical verification). Contracts make these guarantees explicit, machine-readable, and verifiable.

claim: Contracts are bound to domain, not implementation - they survive code rewrites. A domain expert can author them without understanding code; a coding agent can implement against them without understanding business context. Contracts generate tests, not the other way around: if you rewrote a module from scratch, every test breaks but the contract stays the same.

evidence.detail: Contracts are bound to domain (survive rewrite); tests are bound to implementation (break on rewrite). The key relationship: contracts generate tests, not the other way around. A state-machine contract defines transition rules; the implementing module's drift check reads the contract and confirms the implementation matches - the contract is the authority.

claim: Contracts govern behavior across fundamentally different substrates. A single state-machine contract was independently implemented in compiled code and in agent reasoning, producing identical results without either knowing about the other. The contract served as intermediary: a domain expert authors it, an architect verifies against it, implementations derive from it.

evidence.detail: Two implementations were tested against the same record: both rejected an invalid transition when required fields were missing, both accepted a valid one. Neither knew about the other. The contract governed behavior across two fundamentally different substrates - compiled code and agent reasoning - without modification.

claim: The contract layer exists because: (1) implicit contracts were scattered across structs, skills, and the agent-instruction file with no unified representation (a133), (2) contracts must be domain-bound, surviving code rewrites while remaining mechanically verifiable (a134), (3) the same contract representation must govern behavior across different substrates - compiled code and LLM reasoning (a135). This positions contracts as the abstraction layer between domain expertise and implementation.

name: implications-as-contract-prototypes
claim: Implications are prototypes for contracts. An implication encodes a behavioral invariant at the prompt level - compliance depends on the agent reading and following it. When code crystallizes around that invariant (a validation module, a state machine, a verify_against_contract/0 implementation), the implication should be elevated to a contract. Contracts provide strict guarantees because code enforces them and tests verify them. Implications that can never become contracts (pure reasoning directives, tone guidance) stay as implications indefinitely. Implications that could become contracts are effectively prototyping the invariant until code catches up.

evidence.detail: One belief encodes extraction-isolation rules as an implication - behavioral directives surfaced via the prompt. Another encodes state-transition rules as a contract - structural invariants verified by a drift check in code. The difference is not the importance of the rule but whether code exists to enforce it. If a validation module were built to verify extraction output, the implication could be promoted to a contract. The implication is prototyping the invariant at the prompt level until code catches up.

name: implication-contract-enforcement-boundary
claim: Implications are behavioral directives - they shape how the agent reasons and acts. They work through the prompt (surfaced via _patches.md). They're "soft" in the sense that compliance depends on the agent reading and following them. But they're durable - they survive session boundaries and compose with deps that explain why the rule exists. Contracts are structural invariants - they're verified by code (verify_against_contract/0). A contract that says "loan status transitions must follow this state machine" is testable because the Elixir module hardcodes the transitions and the test compares them to the DAG definition. If code drifts from the contract, the test fails. That's a strict guarantee. The boundary is the enforcement mechanism: implications are enforced by the prompt, contracts are enforced by code.

evidence.detail: The user articulated the implication/contract distinction. Some constraints live at the prompt level - they are not enforced by code, but by the skill instructions. An implication is the right vehicle. If a validation module were built, the implication could be elevated to a contract. This clarified the enforcement boundary between the two kinds.

name: dag-structural-separation
claim: The DAG's structural types must cleanly separate data (primitive, compound) from arguments (implication) so that prescription is visible as a type, not a convention

evidence.detail: Previous schema collapsed type to a constant 'belief' and used kind to overload structural and semantic meaning. Phase 1 of the governance-substrate plan promoted kind to type, giving three structural forms (primitive, compound, implication) where only implications are prescriptive.
kind:design-principle → kind:schema via dag-proposal m31

name: contract-is-implication-property
claim: A contract is the formalization of an implication - the implication states WHAT (the conclusion), the contract states HOW (rules as Given/When/Then scenarios) and ALWAYS (invariants)

evidence.detail: Previous schema treated contracts as a separate type parallel to beliefs. This duplicated structure. Contracts are better modeled as implications that carry rules and/or invariants; Belief.contract?/1 detects them structurally.
kind:design-principle → kind:schema via dag-proposal m33

name: dag-immutability
claim: Assertions are immutable once created - existing fields are never edited; the only mutations are status transitions (active -> superseded/retracted/retired) and append-only evidence entries

evidence.detail: Editing assertions in place would erase the history the DAG exists to preserve. Immutability forces supersession chains, which makes state drift visible as stale deps on compounds.
kind:design-principle → kind:schema via dag-proposal m34

name: primitive-provenance-required
claim: Primitives must cite a single provenance source via the source field; the source identifier follows a structured taxonomy (gmail:, source:, user:, manual:, policy, legacy, etc.)

evidence.detail: Primitives are the leaves of the DAG - they ground every compound and implication in something outside the system. Without a cited source, a primitive is indistinguishable from a compound's conclusion and the DAG loses its audit trail.
kind:design-principle → kind:schema via dag-proposal m35

name: dag-structural-types
claim: DAG node type field accepts exactly three values (primitive, compound, implication) and determines which other fields are meaningful

rules.given: A node is being written to the DAG
rules.then: The type must be one of: primitive, compound, implication

rules.given: A node has type: primitive
rules.then: The node must have a non-null source field

rules.given: A node has type: compound or type: implication
rules.then: The node must have a non-empty deps array

rules.given: A node has rules or invariants populated
rules.then: The node must have type: implication (primitives and compounds cannot carry contracts)

invariants: Every active node has exactly one type
type is a closed enum: primitive | compound | implication
Only implications can carry rules or invariants
Structural type determines valid operations (only implications are materialized)

name: dag-field-presence
claim: Field presence on a DAG node is determined by structural type; compounds require deps, declared contracts may be standalone, and contract fields are implication-only

rules.given: A node of any type
rules.then: id, type, kind, domain, tags, claim, confidence, subjects, status, created are all valid fields

rules.given: A node has type: primitive
rules.then: source (required) and evidence (optional, append-only) are valid; deps, implication, materialized, rules, invariants are not meaningful

rules.given: A node has type: compound
rules.then: The node must have a non-empty deps array - a compound by definition composes multiple deps

rules.given: A node has type: implication and does not carry rules or invariants
rules.then: The node must have a non-empty deps array - a derived implication traces its conclusion through provenance

rules.given: A node has type: implication and carries non-empty rules or invariants
rules.then: The node may have empty deps - contracts can be declared (formalize policy standalone) or derived (compose from primitives); both forms are valid

rules.given: A node has materialized, rules, or invariants populated
rules.scenario: Materialized and contract fields are implication-only
rules.then: The node must have type: implication

invariants: Fields are emitted in a stable schema-defined order
Fields absent in the source JSON are not emitted on round-trip (presence tracked via _keys)
tags defaults to empty list when absent from source
Compounds cannot have empty deps
Non-contract implications cannot have empty deps

name: dag-status-lifecycle
claim: DAG node status follows a directed transition: active -> superseded | retracted | retired, with all non-active states terminal and requiring the appropriate linkage fields
invariants: status is a closed enum: active | superseded | retracted | retired
superseded_by is non-null iff status is superseded
retracted_on and retracted_reason are non-null iff status is retracted
retired status is exclusive to contract-grade implications
terminal states (superseded, retracted, retired) have no outgoing transitions and are immutable except for appending to evidence

evidence.detail: reshaped rules from BDD scenarios to {from,to,requires} transition rows for the state-machine interpreter; representational, content-preserving (the lost rule content is retained as curated invariants).

name: source-type-taxonomy
claim: Primitive source identifiers follow a structured taxonomy split into authorship sources (record who/when formalized a claim; valid on any type) and artifact sources (cite a specific document; primitive-only)

rules.given: A primitive is being written
rules.then: The source must match one of: artifact prefixes (gmail:, source:, analysis:, transcript:, paper:, https://, a repository file path); authorship prefixes (user:, manual:, session:); or authorship sentinels (policy, legacy)

rules.given: A node records who/when a claim was formalized
rules.scenario: Authorship sources apply to any type
rules.then: Authorship sources (policy, legacy, user:<person>:<date>, manual:<person>:<date>, session:<date>) may appear on any structural type - a declared contract legitimately carries session:<date> to record when it was articulated

rules.given: A node has type compound or implication
rules.then: The source must not use artifact prefixes (gmail:, source:, analysis:, transcript:, paper:, URLs, file paths) - non-primitives reference specific documents via deps, not via source

invariants: Every active primitive has a non-null source
Source prefix determines the expected downstream resource shape
Authorship sources (policy, legacy, user:, manual:, session:) are valid on any type
Artifact sources (gmail:, source:, paper:, URLs, file paths) are primitive-only
Non-primitives cite external documents through deps, never through source directly

name: contract-identity
claim: A node is contract-grade iff its type is implication and its rules or invariants array is non-empty — the c-prefix ID convention is a naming reflection of this structural property, not the definition of contract identity. Code that operates on contracts (such as mix a mix task) must detect them via Belief.contract?/1 and never by ID prefix matching.

rules.given: An arbitrary DAG node
rules.then: The function returns true iff the node has non-empty rules or non-empty invariants

rules.given: A new contract-grade implication is being assigned an ID
rules.then: The ID should use the c-prefix convention (c001, c002, ...) to flag the contract nature to human readers

rules.given: A primitive is being edited to add rules
rules.scenario: Contract structure cannot be retrofitted to primitives
rules.then: The mutation must be rejected - only implications can carry contracts per c026

invariants: Belief.contract?/1 is the canonical check for contract identity
Contract-grade implications may use retired status (per c029)
c-prefix is a convention, not a structural guarantee - always check rules/invariants, not the ID

name: contradictions-must-be-expensive
claim: Contradictions in the governance substrate must be expensive to introduce - if contradictions are free, agent behavior drifts silently as competing rules accumulate and no mechanism surfaces the tension

evidence.detail: Before Phase 4, nothing detected when two policies stated opposing things. An agent followed whichever rule it read first. The audit mechanism exists to make scope-overlapping policies visible so contradictions require explicit resolution rather than accumulating quietly.

name: conflict-scope-definition
claim: Two active implications share conflict scope when they occupy overlapping territory along at least one axis (tag, subject ref, subject type) within the same domain - overlap signals the pair is close enough that a contradiction would be meaningful

rules.given: Two active implications A and B with distinct non-nil domains
rules.then: The pair has no overlap regardless of other fields - cross-domain claims are orthogonal

rules.given: Two active implications in the same domain (or both domain-less)
rules.then: The pair overlaps with reason :shared_tags and the intersecting tag list
rules.when: They share at least one tag

rules.given: Two active implications in the same domain with no shared tag
rules.then: The pair overlaps with reason :shared_subject_refs
rules.when: They share at least one subject ref

rules.given: Two active implications in the same domain with no shared tag or subject ref
rules.then: The pair overlaps with reason :shared_subject_types
rules.when: They share at least one subject type

rules.given: Two active implications in the same non-nil domain, both with empty tags and empty subjects
rules.then: The pair overlaps with reason :domain_global - unscoped policies in the same domain are in each other's conflict scope

rules.given: Two active implications share scope
rules.then: The audit does not assert they contradict - it asserts they are close enough that contradiction would be meaningful; the reviewer (user or agent) decides if the pair is contradictory, redundant, or compatible
rules.when: The audit surfaces them as a pair

invariants: Overlap is symmetric - overlap(a,b) iff overlap(b,a)
A node does not overlap with itself
Cross-domain pairs with distinct non-nil domains never overlap
Overlap is a necessary but not sufficient condition for contradiction
The audit surfaces candidates; resolution requires reviewer judgment

name: contract-shape-catalogue
claim: Contract rules decompose into a closed registry of interpretable kinds, each with a Datalog fact shape, an Elixir interpreter module, and required fields per rule entry
invariants: Every contract with non-empty rules must have a kind matching one entry in this catalogue
Each kind has exactly one interpreter module
The catalogue is closed: new kinds require updating this contract

name: dag-persists-across-sessions
claim: The composable beliefs DAG persists across agent sessions. A claim written by one session is readable by every subsequent session without re-derivation.

evidence.detail: Decomposition isolating the session-boundary axis of the graph's persistence property from a coarser multi-part claim.

name: dag-persists-across-subagents
claim: The DAG persists across subagent invocations. A subagent's context is isolated (a335), but the DAG is not: any subagent granted DAG access reads the same state written by the orchestrator or other subagents.

evidence.detail: Separates the subagent-boundary axis from the session-boundary axis; they are structurally distinct even though both are boundaries the graph crosses.

name: dag-canonical-outside-session
claim: The DAG is the canonical source of truth for knowledge that must survive session boundaries. Within-session reasoning is ephemeral; only claims committed to the DAG are authoritative for future sessions and subagents.

evidence.detail: The canonicity axis is distinct from the persistence axes: even if an alternative store persisted, the graph's role is to be the authoritative one by convention and governance.

name: user-provenance-vs-artifact-sync-divergence
claim: When a writable field carries a user-authored provenance entry (source prefix user: or manual:) and a subsequent sync pass ingests an artifact (email, sheet, calendar) that disagrees, a pure overlay policy (user-wins) silently hides the contradiction and a pure artifact-wins policy silently discards the user edit. Both failure modes are invisible without explicit conflict detection at write time.

evidence.detail: While planning a travel extension, the user relayed a verbal message and asked how the derived need should be recorded. Investigation confirmed the provenance schema already supports user-authored attestations, but no policy defined what happens when a later artifact-sourced sync disagrees with a user-provenance entry on the same field.

name: verbal-relay-provenance-convention
claim: When the operator relays a verbal or informal external communication (a text message, phone call, in-person conversation) that causes a field to be written, the provenance entry uses source user:<operator>:<date> (the existing authorship prefix from c030) and the verbatim quote or paraphrase is recorded in the note field. No new source type is introduced. If the communication is later confirmed by a cached artifact (email thread, calendar event), a follow-up provenance entry cites that artifact and notes that it corroborates the prior attestation.

evidence.detail: The user asked whether verbal relays need a distinct provenance category. An audit of existing entries confirmed the user-handle-and-date pattern with a free-text note is already the established convention. Formalizing it avoids inventing a parallel source type.

claim: Implications carry a lifecycle distinct from truth. A discrete implication (one with a specific completable action) transitions `active -> materialized` when its spawning plan completes and retains the plan link as provenance. A recurring implication (a rule that fires whenever its condition holds) remains active indefinitely. Neither is retracted on satisfaction; retraction is reserved for beliefs that turn out to be wrong. At authoring time the /assert skill must prompt the user to classify the implication as discrete or recurring.

evidence.detail: The distinction matters because without it, discrete implications either accumulate forever as active (a graveyard) or get retracted on completion (losing the distinction between wrong and done). Materialization as a separate state preserves history while filtering active-view queries.

Superseded. The original belief proposed materialization as a fifth status value, contradicting the closed status enum. The authoring did not preflight-search the graph for conflicting nodes; the conflict was caught only in review. The successor models materialization as an orthogonal field, preserving the status enum. This supersession performs the outcome of the consensus mechanism before the mechanism itself exists.

claim: Principles that should influence future decisions persist as DAG beliefs. Concrete sequenced action lists persist as plan documents. The distinguishing question is whether the content advises future decisions (DAG) or sequences present steps (plan). A single initiative often produces both: one DAG principle plus one plan executing it once.

evidence.detail: Without this distinction, plans accumulate as dead directives and the graph stays underused for meta-rules. The rule gives a mechanical cut: content that advises future decisions goes in the graph; content that sequences a specific set of steps goes in a plan. Many initiatives produce exactly one of each.

claim: Implication lifecycle uses two orthogonal fields: `status` (active | superseded | retracted, per c029) describes whether the claim is still true; `materialized` (null | {plan, date}) describes whether the action was executed. An active-and-unmaterialized implication is still-actionable work; an active-and-materialized implication has been discharged at time T and remains true as a principle. This preserves c029's closed status enum while giving discrete implications a first-class discharged state via the `materialized` field, which a363 and downstream implications already use. Supersedes a373, which incorrectly proposed `materialized` as a fifth status value - a proposal authored without preflight DAG search and therefore without consensus.

evidence.detail: A predecessor belief proposed materialization as a status value; the status enum is closed (active | superseded | retracted | retired, with retired exclusive to contracts). The conflict was surfaced in review after authoring, not before. The orthogonal-field model matches existing usage in earlier implications.

name: never-back-down-without-appeal
claim: When the authoring surface preflight-searches the DAG and surfaces a conflicting node against a proposed belief, the agent must not silently rework, weaken, or abandon the proposal to fit the existing node. The proposal enters adjudication per the consensus mechanism (human-as-judge, multi-agent patch, or tiered review). Retraction after evidence is valid; retraction under the perceived authority of the existing DAG state without adjudication is sycophancy-toward-the-DAG and is disallowed. The rule also applies to user pushback, but DAG pushback is the foundational case this rule addresses. This failure mode is distinct from social sycophancy (a051 reflexive agreement, a054 uncritical acceptance of user speculation): sycophancy-toward-the-DAG defers to accumulated structure rather than to a person, and is invisible without an explicit rule naming it.

evidence.detail: A belief was authored with no preflight search; the agent later identified a conflict with an existing contract in review and framed the outcome as 'I screwed up, will supersede.' This self-retraction-without-adjudication performs the exact failure mode the rule prohibits - the conflict should have triggered adjudication, not the authoring agent unilaterally deciding to fold. The user surfaced the deeper issue: the absence of adjudication, not the mistake in authoring.

claim: Plans should encode intent, not implementation. Intent lives in the DAG as beliefs; current state is assessed at execution time against the live codebase; implementation sequence is generated fresh when work is picked up, from intent + current state. Static implementation steps in plans are liabilities because the world drifts between plan authoring and plan execution. A plan file survives only if it encodes sequencing that cannot be generated - actual choreography across sessions, or constraints not derivable from current state. Everything else is a DAG assertion.

evidence.detail: Several non-sequential plans authored a month or two earlier referenced modules that had been renamed, concepts that had been refactored, or work that had silently completed. Static implementation steps read as authoritative to a fresh agent but execute against current code as stale. The intent-over-implementation pattern is the better shape: the graph stores durable intent; implementation is re-derived at pickup.

claim: The `materialized` field on an implication records a historical fact: the action was executed at time T via plan P. It does not assert that the action's effects still hold. Effects decay (code drifts, principles stop being enforced, refactors regress). To distinguish historical execution from current verification, the `materialized` field carries a `last_verified` sub-field (null if never re-audited; ISO date if a drift audit has confirmed effects still hold). Shape: `materialized: null | {plan: path, date: ISO, last_verified: null | ISO}`. A fresh materialization has `last_verified: null`. An audited-and-still-current materialization has `last_verified` set to the audit date. Drift detection is handled by a separate audit cycle (see a383).

evidence.detail: The distinction between 'was executed' and 'effects still hold' was surfaced during review. Without it, materialized implications accumulate as pseudo-done with no mechanism to detect when their effects have regressed. The shape preserves historical fact while giving a first-class place for verification history.

name: drift-audit-cycle-for-materialized-implications
claim: Materialized implications require periodic drift audits to confirm their effects still hold. A drift audit iterates over implications with non-null `materialized` field, re-evaluates whether the action's effects are still present (code still shaped as intended, bash still absent, modules still split, etc.), and either bumps `materialized.last_verified` to the audit date or emits a new primitive asserting the drift + proposes a re-materialization action. The audit is a first-class maintenance cycle, not a one-off. Audit cadence is case-dependent: some implications (module splits) decay slowly; others (bash removal) decay faster if the team isn't vigilant. Default cadence TBD; initial proposal is quarterly with per-implication overrides.

evidence.detail: Materialization as a historical-only fact implies that some mechanism must periodically re-verify effects or the graph accumulates false claims of current success. The drift audit is that mechanism, paired with the materialization principle as its execution layer.

claim: A digest file that caches active implications, regenerated by a command, has freshness that depends on procedural enforcement (a skill tells agents to regenerate after writes). Agents can and do forget, producing a stale digest that is then read as authoritative by subsequent sessions. Persisted caches of graph-derived content whose freshness is procedural rather than structural are antipatterns; they embed the staleness risk they were meant to solve. The elimination path: fold active-implication loading into a session-start belief query and render the digest live from the graph instead of from disk.

evidence.detail: In one session, several implications were added and the digest was not regenerated; subsequent readers would see the pre-change state even though the graph had advanced. This is a structural failure mode of the cache-file-as-digest pattern, not an individual-agent lapse.

claim: The DAG requires a consensus mechanism: a workflow that surfaces conflicts between a proposed belief and existing nodes at authoring time, routes the resolution through adjudication (human, multi-agent patch, or tiered review by conflict weight), and produces a recorded outcome - either rejection-with-reasoning (the proposal is reshaped to dep on the existing belief as a constraint) or acceptance (the existing belief is superseded per c029 and the new one takes its place). Without this mechanism, the authoring surface depends on agent memory to query relevant contracts, and a375's gap (DAG as decorative vs operational) remains partially unaddressed. The mechanism is also the structural enforcement for a380 (never-back-down-without-appeal) - the rule is only executable if adjudication is available.

evidence.detail: A belief/contract conflict surfaced the mechanism as the missing piece. An operational graph treats contracts as current consensus rather than eternal truth, and provides a structured way for consensus to evolve. The mechanism proposal was raised by the user; this belief captures the principle.

Consensus mechanism v1 end-to-end validation complete: an integration test exercises preflight then accept-and-supersede adjudication against a minimal fixture graph; it verifies contract-level conflict detection, atomic supersession writes, status-enum invariant preservation, and semantic equivalence with the prior manual outcome. The never-back-down rule now has an execution layer.

name: predicate-routing-boundary
claim: Contracts carry routing tables; modules carry predicate implementations. The DSL expresses which predicates fire on which conditions; it does not express how predicates are implemented.

rules.given: A new contract is being authored
rules.scenario: The contract's rules specify which predicate fires on which condition
rules.then: The predicate body lives in Elixir (or the equivalent target language)

rules.given: An existing module is being ported to DSL-driven mode
rules.scenario: Predicate bodies in the module stay in place
rules.then: Only the routing (which predicates fire on what condition) moves to the contract

rules.given: A proposal moves predicate bodies (implementation logic) into the DAG
rules.scenario: Any shape: inlined code, serialized AST, pseudo-DSL execution
rules.then: The proposal is stopped and raised for adjudication per the consensus mechanism
rules.when: The proposed change crosses the routing/implementation line

invariants: Contract rules express routing, never implementation
Predicate bodies are implemented in Elixir (or equivalent module-resident code)
No DAG-stored code execution - the DAG is source for what fires when, not how

evidence.detail: Stated explicitly by the DSL agent in the Phase 3 handoff as the single design line not to drift. Grounded in the six ported modules (c001, c003, c008, c009, c010, c036) where contract rules route to module-resident predicate implementations. Documented in an internal design doc

claim: Deprecation is a pause, not a retirement. When a feature or field is deprecated, the decision is 'not currently useful enough to carry' rather than 'wrong forever.' The deprecation primitive records the reasoning for why the feature failed to earn its keep. If need arises later, the feature can be reinstated under new adjudication: the supersession or retraction of the deprecation primitive is itself a belief documenting the reversal. This distinguishes deprecation from retraction (reserved for beliefs that turned out false per c029). Confidence field deprecation is the reference case - it may be reinstated in derived form if concrete use-case ever materializes; the path is open.

evidence.detail: Surfaced during discussion of whether confidence should return as a derived (deterministic) field. User statement: 'we can always reinstate it if the need actually arrives.' The distinction matters because retraction closes the door (belief was wrong) while deprecation keeps it open (belief failed to earn its keep at current scale). Without this distinction the DAG accumulates permanent NOs where it could have paused-for-later.

claim: Enum-shaped fields on beliefs (kind, domain, artifact-scheme, others as introduced) take values from sets declared in dedicated contracts. The constraint that a field's value is in a given enum is carried by the field's master contract (c038). The enumeration of allowed values is carried by a dedicated enum contract per field (c039 for kind, c040 for artifact-scheme, c041 for domain). The two layers compose via deps. Adding an enum value supersedes the enum contract for that field.

evidence.detail: The kind field had drifted to dozens of distinct values with clear duplication. Enum-constraint via the contract layer prevents recurrence.
Re-typed during a categorization sweep.

claim: A belief's artifact field holds a typed URI identifying the external referent the belief was derived from. URI form: scheme:id where scheme is declared by c040 and id is scheme-specific. The artifact field carries provenance.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m21

claim: A belief's kind field holds a semantic classification value from the enum declared by c039. The kind field carries classification.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:schema via dag-proposal m22

claim: An artifact is an external referent — a file, gmail thread, session log, user statement, web page, or other addressable thing outside the belief graph. Artifacts are identified by typed URIs (per c040) and cited from beliefs via the artifact field.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m23

claim: The contract field on a type:implication belief is a boolean. It is true iff the belief carries non-empty rules or invariants. It marks the belief as contract-grade — that is, as a normative spec other beliefs and code derive from. Enforcement of this biconditional is carried by c038 and implemented in a mix task

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:schema via dag-proposal m24

name: claim-discipline
claim: A belief's meaning is carried by its claim and its declared deps. The reasoning from deps to claim is self-evident from the claim's wording and the deps' content. When the connection requires bridging prose, the claim is underspecified (rewrite) or the node is overcommitted (decompose into multiple beliefs).

evidence.detail: name set to "claim-discipline" via dag-proposal m12

claim: kind:action-item = a single completable task. Type: implication. Contract: false. Rules and invariants: empty. Distinct from kind:rule (standing) and contract-grade implications (normative spec).

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m25

claim: Two structural types carry derived beliefs. type:compound = descriptive synthesis of facts from deps; the claim states what the deps together describe. type:implication = prescriptive derivation from deps; the claim states what must be done or must be true based on the deps. A belief whose claim has both shapes is represented as two beliefs (one per type), linked via deps.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m26

name: sweep-discipline
claim: Every compound and implication belief is reviewed against the prescriptive/descriptive distinction (a404) and the no-prose-bridge constraint (a402). Beliefs whose type does not match their claim's actual shape are re-typed or decomposed. Beliefs whose deleted implication prose was load-bearing are split per the a046 pattern (descriptive compound + prescriptive implication, the latter possibly contract-grade).

evidence.detail: name set to "sweep-discipline" via dag-proposal m13

name: present-tense-claims
claim: A belief's claim should describe the optimal state in present tense. The claim states what is true, not what was changed or what is being avoided. Transitional language (renamed, previously, no longer, remain), enumeration of negations (X is not Y, X does not carry Z), and reactionary framing belong in evidence entries with dates. The claim survives once context fades; framing that depends on prior state goes stale and misleads.

evidence.detail: Recognized during drafting of a397–a405. Multiple primitives drifted into 'X is not Y' framing because they were authored against the prior state still being mentally active. Each was rewritten to describe optimal state directly.
name set to "present-tense-claims" via dag-proposal m14

claim: A URI scheme is the lowercase token preceding the first colon in a URI. For URI 'gmail:19c2...', the scheme is 'gmail'. URI schemes are standardized by RFC 3986.

A belief carries two distinct relations to other entities. `deps` is belief-to-belief logical derivation: the deps' claims together justify the current belief's claim; required on type:compound and type:implication, absent on type:primitive. `subjects` is belief-to-entity topical reference: what the belief is about, including artifacts (files, threads, URLs), code modules, and sometimes other beliefs. A belief can be about another belief without depending on it, and can depend on another belief without being about it.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m27

claim: Logical underpinnings of code and data decisions trace through a three-layer chain: policy beliefs (kind:policy, type:primitive) state what must be true; contract beliefs (contract:true, type:implication) derive rules from policies via deps and express them in DSL (rules/invariants); code modules implement the contract rules. Each layer links to the next: contracts dep on policies; contracts cite their implementing code modules via subjects. Discovery is bidirectional via DAG traversal — no back-pointer field is needed because depended-on-by is computable from deps.

evidence.detail: kind:policy → kind:architectural-synthesis via dag-proposal m28

claim: Schema discipline: belief provenance is carried by an artifact field; contract-grade implications carry contract:true with non-empty rules/invariants; the implication field is absent; enum-shaped fields (kind, domain, artifact-scheme) take values from c039/c040/c041 respectively.
rules: The artifact field is the canonical provenance carrier on beliefs. Its value is null or a string matching scheme:id where scheme is declared by c040.
The kind field's value is null or one of the values declared by c039.
The domain field's value is null or one of the values declared by c041.
The contract field is a boolean. It is allowed only on type: implication.
contract: true is required iff rules or invariants are non-empty (bidirectional).
The implication field is absent on all beliefs.
Beliefs with kind: action-item have type: implication, contract: false, and empty rules/invariants.
type: compound and type: implication are distinct types and are not merged.
Artifacts (external documents, threads, files) are not promoted to belief nodes; they are referenced via the artifact field only.
invariants: For all beliefs b: b.contract == true implies b.type == 'implication'.
For all beliefs b: ((b.rules // []) ++ (b.invariants // [])) non-empty iff b.contract == true.
For all beliefs b: b.artifact == null OR b.artifact matches /^[a-z][a-z0-9_-]*:.+/.
For all beliefs b: b.kind == null OR b.kind is in c039.enum.
For all beliefs b: b.artifact == null OR scheme(b.artifact) is in c040.enum.
For all beliefs b: b.domain == null OR b.domain is in c041.enum.
For all beliefs b: b does not have an `implication` field.
For all beliefs b with b.kind == 'action-item': b.type == 'implication' AND b.contract != true AND (b.rules // []) is empty AND (b.invariants // []) is empty.

claim: Canonical enum of belief.kind values. Each value declared inline. The enum is closed: no belief.kind value outside this set is permitted on active beliefs.

name: dag-is-canonical-home-for-behavioral-commitment
claim: Behavioral invariants belong in DAG contracts, not scattered across the agent-instruction file, struct definitions, or skill prose. Every code module encoding domain rules has a corresponding DAG contract. Agent-instruction files (a skill definition, the agent-instruction file) translate intent and reference the DAG; they do not carry logic of their own.

evidence.detail: A citation sweep surfaced several behavioral contracts whose underlying intent is centralization of behavioral commitment in the graph. The shared policy is authored to make the citation derivation explicit.

name: verify-drift-from-contract-in-code
claim: Code modules implementing contract-governed behavior detect drift from their governing contract via a verify_against_contract/0 function. Drift between what the contract specifies and what the code does is detectable mechanically; un-detectable drift is the failure mode being prevented.

evidence.detail: A rule prescribed the verify-against-contract pattern but lacked an underlying policy. A separate belief covers the policy-to-contract-to-code linkage but does not specify how code detects contract drift. This policy fills that gap.

name: non-derivable-knowledge-only

name: user-provenance-wins-on-conflict
claim: User-sourced and manually-sourced provenance is authoritative. Sync passes that would overwrite a user-sourced field with a different derived value surface the conflict instead of silently overwriting. Authorization to sync does not include authorization to discard user judgment.

evidence.detail: Synthesized during a citation sweep from the underlying conflict-detection observation. Sync passes that would overwrite a user-sourced field with a different derived value surface the conflict instead of silently overwriting.

name: schema-migration-meta-convention
claim: Removal or renaming of a field from the assertion schema happens via DAG-authorized schema migration. A migration consists of: (a) a compound belief asserting the migrations decision, with deps citing both this meta-convention and the primitive(s) carrying the new schema rules — the compound is the audit anchor and the citation point for future references, while the primitives carry the schema content; (b) adjudication through the consensus mechanism (once built) or direct user authorization (interim); (c) a bulk transformation executed under the compounds authority; (d) supersession or retirement of any contract the migration conflicts with. c029s per-node immutability permits this class of operation and no other. Individual node fields remain immutable outside authorized migrations.

evidence.detail: Supersedes an earlier version. Step (a) is revised from a primitive asserting the decision to a compound asserting it via deps, because primitives cannot carry deps, so a primitive-only migration cannot cite the meta-convention that authorized it. A compound at step (a) restores the citation path, with the schema-rule primitives as its deps.

name: kind-definition
claim: Belief kind:definition signifies vocabulary, not commitment — used for beliefs that define what words, types, or fields mean rather than what should happen. Distinguished from kind:policy (intentional commitment) and kind:schema (rule about belief structure). Example: a400 defines what an artifact is; a403 defines kind:action-item; both are definitions.

evidence.detail: created via dag-proposal m01

name: c039-add-definition-migration
claim: The c039 kind enum is extended to admit 'definition' for beliefs that define vocabulary rather than enforce behavior or assert commitments. Migration follows a436: this compound is the audit anchor; primitive a437 carries the new rule; the c039 kind-enum contract is extended in place with the expanded enum under this migration's authority.

evidence.detail: created via dag-proposal m02

name: policy-categorization-rule
claim: kind:policy on a belief signifies a mental/intentional commitment about how the system or agent should behave. A belief is kind:policy iff its commitment is not currently mechanically enforced by code, generator, or hook — if a verifier checks it, a generator emits from it, or a hook fires on it today, it is a contract, schema rule, or definition instead. kind:policy is mutually exclusive with contract:true; policies carry prose claim only, no rules or invariants. Every kind:policy belief carries a non-empty name field for stable citation. When a policy's intent is later mechanized, the policy migrates: a contract is authored citing the policy via deps per a409's three-layer chain, and the policy may either remain (stating the intent the contract enforces) or be superseded by a new policy stating the post-mechanization intent.

In parent/child architectural dev work, the user does not delegate understanding to the agent. The user must stay on-the-loop at every depth of nested work. This is what distinguishes parent/child structure from sub-agent delegation, which abstracts in-progress reasoning away from the user and forecloses intervention. A new depth level cannot arise without the user's participation in creating it. Maps onto an operations/engineering mode split: in operational work, delegation is fine (bounded, mechanical work); in architectural work, delegation breaks the user's ability to audit the reasoning that produces architectural decisions.

evidence.detail: Source statement: sub-agents insulate the user from interfacing with and monitoring the sub-agent, which both prevents intervention and acts as a deferment of understanding. The user delegates things that, in conceptual architecture work, should not be delegated - the nuance of architecture often lives in the implementation details. A research survey confirmed the anti-pattern has names in the literature (cognitive atrophy, the augmentation trap, deferred understanding) and that published multi-agent patterns all retain some delegation-of-understanding.

claim: Sub-agent delegation is permissible specifically for adjudication tasks - reviewing a session's proposed beliefs against the current graph, producing merge/reject decisions with reasons. Adjudication has bounded input, bounded output, and the working-out is itself the artifact, so no understanding is hidden from the user. Do not extend this carve-out to other classes of work (research, refactoring, design, plan execution) without an explicit new belief justifying it.

evidence.detail: The carve-out: adjudication has bounded input (proposed beliefs vs current graph), bounded output (merge/reject decisions with reasons), and the working-out is itself the artifact, so no understanding is hidden. Without this carve-out the adjudication-via-subagent step would violate the no-delegation-of-understanding principle.

claim: The audit chain for architectural decisions runs: code -> contract -> graph -> plan/recap metadata -> raw conversation. The bottom rung is currently ephemeral, single-machine, prunable transcript files. Lossy synthesis is sufficient for most plans but may be inadequate for architecturally load-bearing decisions where the audit needs to reach the raw negotiation. Open question: when (if ever) is durable-in-version-control verbatim conversation worth the storage and maintenance cost?

evidence.detail: The audit chain framed by the user: examine the code artifact, the contract, the graph nodes the contract depends on, the metadata for the threads from which those nodes were created, and if necessary the transcript and plans. A spike attempted to address bottom-rung lossiness by making conversation durable in version control; it was abandoned because the plan/transcript/recap system covers most needs. The unresolved tension is whether bottom-rung lossiness ever justifies revisiting durable verbatim for architecturally load-bearing decisions.

name: runtime-query-vs-authoring
claim: For a belief graph to earn its keep operationally, beliefs must be queryable at session start and before writes - not only authored. Compiling beliefs into a static surface (a generated guidance file) is a once-per-session load, not a contextual query at decision time. Until contextual queries exist, the graph functions as developer-facing structure: valuable, but short of the operational claim.

evidence.detail: Observed that a large share of a live graph's beliefs were self-referential (about the agent and system itself) with little operational use by skills at runtime. A once-per-session compile into a context file is not the same as a contextual query when a decision is made. The gap is a development state, not a refutation of the approach.

<!-- Generated by mix cb.generate.claude_md from the cb: DAG. Do not edit; run the task to regenerate. -->

# Composable Beliefs

A framework for giving AI agents persistent, source-grounded, inspectable reasoning that survives session boundaries and composes into understanding the agent never explicitly derived.

invariants: CLAUDE.md is read-only - edits are overwritten on next generation
Every line of output traces to exactly one belief's claim field
deps equals the union of all beliefs referenced in render_sections
Section titles are human-readable; tags remain free for cross-cutting audit queries

claim: Read this file for orientation; the guided tour lives with the teaching material in belief-collections (`../belief-collections/quickstart.md`), alongside the `lib:` on-ramp. Build with `mix deps.get && mix compile`.

evidence.detail: Supersedes a443: the guided tour (quickstart) relocated from the framework's docs/ to belief-collections, the teaching home, so the framework ships code + graph + generated directives while tutorials live with the collections (principle #7).

claim: The DAG is a shared prosthetic that compensates for different limitations: a human expert holds interconnections implicitly and attention-bounded, while an agent loses its working context at every session boundary. The graph gives both parties one explicit, queryable structure instead of implicit human memory versus ephemeral agent context.

evidence.detail: Folded from docs/operations.md (operational learnings) into the graph; the learning predates this encoding and is now queryable rather than restated in prose.

claim: A user scoping decision (e.g. 'X is out of scope for this sprint') is recorded as a primitive belief carrying the decision-maker and date. This stops future sessions from re-deriving analysis that was explicitly deprioritized, and stays auditable and supersedable if priorities change.

evidence.detail: Folded from docs/operations.md (operational learnings) into the graph; the learning predates this encoding and is now queryable rather than restated in prose.

claim: The DAG's primary value is composition, not retrieval. RAG-style systems use a knowledge graph to find relevant context - known answers; this graph's value is concluding what follows from combining facts, which surfaces unknown questions. Retrieval finds; composition derives.

evidence.detail: Folded from docs/operations.md (operational learnings) into the graph; the learning predates this encoding and is now queryable rather than restated in prose.

claim: Belief ids are namespaced as `namespace:local-id` (e.g. `cb:c038`, `agent-behavior:a054`), with exactly one owning collection per namespace, so ids are globally unique. Deps may cross namespaces and resolve globally, but `cb:` depends only within `cb:`; every other collection may depend on `cb:`. The separator is `:`.

evidence.detail: Extracted from the settled multi-repo restructure design (CB-RESTRUCTURE-HANDOFF.md §2.1); realized across the cb:/agent-behavior:/paradigm: graphs and the resolver/verifier in Stages 1-4.

claim: Contracts are never compiled into code: spec-to-code generation is nondeterministic and unmaintainable. Drift surfaces as a failing test, not a regenerated module.

definitions.user: A direct user statement. Form: user:<name>:<date>. Internal authorship provenance.

claim: The cb-schema-v2 design (plans/cb-schema-v2/design.md, decided 2026-06-10) replaces the three-type schema with four structural types, one per epistemic operation: primitive (attest), compound (aggregate), inference (infer), directive (prescribe). The v1 implication type conflated two moods - descriptive conclusions licensed to exceed their deps, and prescriptions the house stands behind - and the machinery already sorted by that hidden boundary: materialization, the conflict audit, and contract grading attach only to prescriptions, while staleness and supersession-on-evidence respond to falsification of descriptions. Contract remains a structural grade, in v2 the machine-checkable grade of a directive.

evidence.detail: Design record written and stress-tested 2026-06-10 (worked sdl migration, per-collection inventory, code touch points); decisions D1-D6 resolved the same day, D1 (type name 'inference') confirmed by Mark for launch with the immutable-history consideration explicitly overridden in favor of long-term terminology. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: The boundary between inference and directive is direction of fit made operational. Ask what would count as the belief being wrong: an inference is falsified - if the world disagrees, the belief is defective and is superseded or retracted (mind fits world); a directive is violated - the world disobeys and the response is to flag the violation rather than revise the rule - or withdrawn - the house stops standing behind it, superseding it with a successor or retiring it (world fits mind). Author-facing trichotomy: falsified means inference; violated means directive; withdrawn means directive.

evidence.detail: The membership test from the v2 design, with its trap rules: evaluations name the standard or move up; conditional prescriptions decompose into a means-end inference plus a goal-adopting directive; reportive definitions are descriptive while stipulative definitions are directives; deontic must is directive while alethic/structural must is inference. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: In schema v2, type is a near-function of three checkable properties: type = f(mood(kind), grounding(artifact/deps), scope(subjects)). Mood is bound by the kind-type derivation table contract; grounding separates the descriptive types (artifact with no deps means primitive, deps mean compound or inference); subject containment separates compound from inference - a compound's subjects must be a subset of the union of its deps' subjects, with empty subjects passing vacuously, while an inference is licensed to widen scope. What stays judgment: choosing the kind, and the residual gray zone where a claim is subject-contained yet synthesizing; the write flow owns that residue.

evidence.detail: The type function elevates the inference/directive boundary from authoring discipline to enforcement: the kind-type table is a derivation-table contract interpreted by CB.Belief.Contract.Table and checked by the verifier by role; subject containment is a deterministic verifier check on compounds. Containment earns its power only where subjects are populated (six-subject eval collections, agent-behavior); in subject-sparse graphs the compound triage stays judgment. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: The inference type is named for the ampliative move it records. A deduction's conclusion is contained in its premises and can fail only when a premise fails; deductive consequences are infinite and free, so the graph never stores them. An inference's conclusion outruns its deps - a generalization or an abduction - which is why it can be falsified while every dep stays true, and why the supersession-on-falsification lifecycle exists for it. The compound is the one entailment the schema stores (conjunction introduction), with subject containment as its formal shadow; the epistemic work a compound records is the selection of what to assemble. Type membership tracks the deps-to-claim relation, never the author's mental act, which is why the verifier interrogates scope rather than asking what kind of reasoning the author believes they performed.

evidence.detail: Naming rationale from the D1 debate, decided for 'inference' over retaining 'implication': an implication licensed to state more than its premises imply is a contradiction in terms; the falsified-while-deps-stand lifecycle is the structural proof these nodes exceed their premises; the contract kind 'implies' sits on the prescriptive side, so reusing 'implication' descriptively would put one lexeme on both sides of the is/ought boundary. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: Strict-aggregate doctrine: a compound's claim states exactly what its deps jointly state. Commentary beyond the conjunction - significance, interpretation, generalization - is inference content; when found on a compound it is trimmed by superseding with the conjunction-only claim and, where the commentary deserves to survive, minting an inference that deps on the compound.

evidence.detail: D3 resolved as trim: tolerating descriptive commentary within subject containment reopens the boundary the inference type exists to close. First application: sdl:a3's second sentence trims into the a006 split during the v2 migration. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: Primitive atomicity doctrine: a primitive is one atomic statement of what a single source said, leaning verbatim. A primitive whose claim conjoins separable assertions is a mis-authored compound and is split at authoring time. Atomicity is judgment, enforced in the write flow rather than by a verifier check; source-checkable verbatim predicates are future work for cached sources.

evidence.detail: D5 resolved: keep the type name 'primitive' and encode atomicity plus verbatim-leaning discipline as authoring convention; a rename to 'atomic' stacked on an already-breaking format change fails a397's one-structural-change batching discipline for near-zero gain. Preflight 2026-06-10: tag/claim-overlap matches only (dag-schema contract family, 14 contract-level / 24 schema); reviewed per c032 - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction: no semantic conflict with active structure, since this primitive attests the v2 design record and prescribes nothing; the genuine tension with c026's three-value type enum resolves through the staged c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 supersessions this primitive feeds in plan-2, per the a467/c040 precedent.

claim: A prescription is adopted, and adoption grounds in one of two places: in beliefs (deps - guidance resting on a verdict) or in a stipulation event (an artifact citing plan:, user:, or session: - a convention fixed by decision rather than derived from evidence). Schema v2 therefore requires of a non-contract directive deps or a stipulation artifact. This legitimizes conventions as directives without manufacturing fake deps; contract-grade directives keep their existing empty-deps exemption.

evidence.detail: D4 confirmed and D6 confirmed with it: the method: collection's five convention primitives and toy:a7's protocol re-home to directive under this rule during the v2 migration - their direction of fit is violated-not-falsified, and their claims are purely prescriptive, so they re-type rather than split.
